title: Paths of light rays through spherical lenses
description: Let's practice some problems to better understand the paths that light rays take after refracting through spherical lenses.
topic_path: Paths of light rays through spherical lenses

assessment_items.question_content:
A ray of light is incident on a convex lens parallel to the principal axis, as shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the path of the ray after it passes through the lens?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

Rays that enter a convex lens parallel to the principal axis converge at a single point after passing through it. 

Can you recall which point is that exactly?

##Solution

A ray entering a convex lens parallel to its principal axis passes through the focus, $\text{F}_2$, after refraction.

[[☃ image 1]]

The correct answer is:

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c36d94f7a97503af7a24ee3829ed224a.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
A ray of light goes through the focus of a convex lens after refraction.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the path of the incident ray?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

Light rays that fall on a convex lens parallel to the principal axis converge at its focus. 

Can we use this to guess the answer?

##Solution

The incident ray is parallel to the principal axis.

[[☃ image 1]]

The correct answer is:

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6eacf6dc34335a13549ab25cdf7913c5.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
A ray of light falls on a convex lens after passing through the first principal focus, $\text F_1$, as shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the path of the ray after it passes through the lens?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

If we place a tiny light source at the first principal focus of a convex lens, $\text F_1$, we'll get a straight beam of light parallel to the principal axis.

[[☃ image 1]]

What does this tell us about a single ray of light incident through $\text F_1$?

##Solution

A ray entering a convex lens after passing through $\text F_1$ emerges parallel to the principal axis after refraction.

[[☃ image 1]]

The correct answer is:

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a041a263b996372c480345f96c81324c.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
A ray of light emerges parallel to the principal axis after passing through a convex lens, as shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the path of the incident ray?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

If we place a tiny light source at the first principal focus of a convex lens, $\text F_1$, we'll get a straight beam of light parallel to the principal axis.

[[☃ image 1]]

What does this tell us about a single ray of light that emerges parallel to the principal axis after refraction?

##Solution

A ray entering a convex lens after passing through $\text F_1$ emerges parallel to the principal axis after refraction.

[[☃ image 1]]

The correct answer is:

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ee9c54f8f471bc9314ab053b16ea2b8c.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
A ray of light is incident on a convex lens in the direction of the optical center, $\text O$, as shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the path of the ray after it passes through the lens?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

A very special thing about the optical center, $\text O$, of a lens is that a ray of light passing through it undergoes no deviation.

##Solution

The emergent ray is along the same direction as the incident ray.

[[☃ image 1]]

The correct answer is:

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/34c643c2346a5d895d2dac46370362c4.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
A ray of light emerges in the direction away from the optical center, $\text O$, after refraction through a convex lens, as shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the path of the incident ray?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

A very special thing about the optical center, $\text O$, of a lens is that a ray of light passing through it undergoes no deviation.

##Solution

The incident ray is in the same direction as the emergent ray.

[[☃ image 1]]

The correct answer is:

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d5e25063a4b88b7060d0f9b47293e6e0.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
A ray of light is incident on a concave lens parallel to the principal axis, as shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the path of the ray after it passes through the lens?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

Rays that enter a concave lens parallel to the principal axis appear to diverge from a single point on the principal axis.

[[☃ image 1]]

Can you recall which point that is, exactly?

##Solution

A ray of light entering a concave lens parallel to its principal axis appears to refract away from the first principal focus, $\text F_1$, of the lens.

[[☃ image 1]]

The correct answer is:

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/809035f140e9b2c5dec433cf8722ae60.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
A ray of light emerges in the direction away from the first principal focus, $\text F_1$, after passing through a concave lens, as shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the path of the incident ray?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

Rays that enter a concave lens parallel to the principal axis appear to diverge from a single point on the principal axis.

[[☃ image 2]]

Can you recall which point that is, exactly?

##Solution

A ray of light entering a concave lens parallel to its principal axis appears to refract away from the first principal focus, $\text F_1$, of the lens.

[[☃ image 1]]

The correct answer is:

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c9f7ff10e4241ec00c412f865e3e7be2.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
A ray of light is incident on a concave lens in the direction toward the second principal focus, $\text F_2$, as shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the path of the ray after it passes through the lens?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

Rays that enter a concave lens in the direction toward $\text F_2$ emerge parallel to the principal axis.

##Solution

The emergent ray is parallel to the principal axis, as shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

The correct answer is:

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/51e02671c82043de67fb7fe2322b9145.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
A ray of light emerges parallel to the principal axis after refraction through a concave lens, as shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the path of the incident ray?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

Rays that enter a concave lens in the direction toward $\text F_2$ emerge parallel to the principal axis.

##Solution

The incident ray is in the direction toward the second principal focus, $\text F_2$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The correct answer is:

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a3f17cea4cddd9653cd01d6076cfb8ee.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
A ray of light is incident on a concave lens in the direction toward the optical center, $\text O$, as shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the path of the ray after it passes through the lens?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

A very special thing about the optical center, $\text O$, of a lens is that a ray of light passing through it undergoes no deviation.

##Solution

The emergent ray is along the same direction as the incident ray.

[[☃ image 1]]

The correct answer is:

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2850ce29ae01955bf9521f663bf4affa.png)

assessment_items.question_content:
A ray of light emerges in the direction away from the optical center, $\text O$, after refraction through a concave lens, as shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the path of the incident ray?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

Rays that enter a convex lens parallel to the principal axis converge at a single point after passing through it. 

Can you recall which point is that exactly?

##Solution

The incident ray is in the same direction as the emergent ray.

[[☃ image 1]]

The correct answer is:

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f75ef61894c8f086a909d55a2abc7311.png)
